perturbation.original_text: As used in this Agreement, "Confidential Informat ion" refers to any information which entails personal details of fello w members, supporters, associates such as names, ad dresses, telephone numbers and other contact details as well as operational manuals, methods, computer software , works of art, drafts and designs, financial data, p laces of meetings, secrets and other proprietary in formation related to the past, current, future and proposed a ctivities of the Society, and any other informatio n which is privileged, proprietary and confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: As used in this Agreement, "Confidential Informat ion" refers to any information which entails personal details of fello w members, supporters, associates such as names, ad dresses, telephone numbers and other contact details as well as operational manuals, methods, computer software , works of art, drafts and designs, financial data, p laces of meetings, secrets and other proprietary in formation related to the past, current, future and proposed a ctivities of the Society, and any other informatio n.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'which is privileged, proprietary and confidential', the definition of Confidential Information becomes less precise and potentially conflicts with the legal standard for trade secrets under Australian law, which requires information to be confidential to be protected.
perturbation.law_citation: Commonwealth of Australia - Trade Practices Act 1974 (repealed)
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Australian law, trade secrets require an element of confidentiality and that the information gives the confider (Q Society of Australia) a competitive edge. By making the definition less precise, the legal basis for protecting information as a trade secret is weakened. Removing the privileged, proprietary and confidential qualification weakens that protection and could make it harder to enforce. The Act was repealed, but the common law principles regarding trade secrets still require demonstrable confidential qualities for protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Customs Tariff Amendment Act (No. 2) 1993 - Federal Register of Legislation Skip to main Text Details Authorises Downloads All versions Interactions Customs Tariff Amendment Act (No. 2) 1993 No longer in force Click for more info Administered by Immigration and Border Protection Latest version Order print copy Save this title to My Account Set up an alert C2004A04654 01 January 1988 - 21 December 1993 Legislation text View document Select value Act Filter active Table of contents Enter text to search the table of contents Customs Tariff Amendment Act (No. 2) 1993 - [Act]
perturbation.justification: The change removes the explicit requirement that the information be 'privileged, proprietary, and confidential,' weakening the legal basis for protecting the information as a trade secret under Australian law. The Perturbation Explanation states this can make it harder to enforce protection. The Law Explanation further clarifies that trade secrets under Australian law require an element of confidentiality to give a competitive edge. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly relevant as it pertains to customs tariff amendment act, the Perturbation and Law Explanations sufficiently demonstrate that removing the phrase 'which is privileged, proprietary and confidential' weakens the protection of trade secrets. This aligns with the Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction definition, where contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient will maintain in confidence and will no t disclose, disseminate or use any Confidential Infor mation belonging to and/or related to Society, whet her or not in written form. Recipient agrees that Recipient s hall treat all Confidential Information of the Soci ety with at least the same degree of care as Recipient accords its own confidential information. Recipient furthe r represents that Recipient exercises at least reason able care to protect its own confidential informati on.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient will maintain in confidence and will no t disclose, disseminate or use any Confidential Infor mation belonging to and/or related to Society, whet her or not in written form. Recipient agrees that Recipient s hall treat all Confidential Information of the Soci ety with at least the same degree of care as Recipient deems reasonable. Recipient furthe r represents that Recipient exercises at least reason able care to protect its own confidential informati on.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the phrase 'same degree of care as Recipient accords its own confidential information' with 'degree of care as Recipient deems reasonable', the standard of care is subjective rather than objective. This could conflict with implied duties under Australian contract law to act in good faith and with reasonable care.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith (Australian Contract Law)
perturbation.law_citation: Commonwealth of Australia - Competition and Consumer Act 2010
perturbation.law_explanation: The Act outlines that all parties must act reasonably. Changing the wording to 'degree of care as Recipient deems reasonable', puts the power in the Recipient's hand, which can violate the implied duty of good faith and reasonable care in contractual performance under Australian common law. This introduces ambiguity as to whether the recipient's self-defined standard is adequate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Legislation we enforce | ACCC Skip to content Skip to navigation Skip to search Warning: We've had reports of scammers using ACCC phone numbers to contact members of the public. They do this to steal personal information. The ACCC does not make calls from our reception numbers. If you receive a call claiming to be from the ACCC asking for personal information, do not provide this information and hang up. This issue is being investigated. Close Search Menu Search (required) Search Listen Legislation we enforce The ACCC is an independent statutory authority that enforces the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (previously the Trade Practices Act 1974 ) and other legislation. On this page The Competition and Consumer Act 2010 The Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (CCA) covers most areas of the market: the relationships between suppliers, wholesalers, retailers, and consumers. Its purpose is to enhance the welfare of Australians by promoting fair trading and competition, and through the provision of consumer protections. Broadly, it covers: product safety and labelling unfair market practices price monitoring industry codes industry regulation – airports, electricity, gas, telecommunications mergers and acquisitions. Main sections of the Act Part IIIA - third party access to nationally significant, essential facilities Part IV - anti-competitive practices Part IVB - industry codes of conduct Part VI - enforcement and remedies Part VII - authorisations, notifications and class exemptions Part VIIA - price monitoring and surveillance relating to industries or businesses declared by the Australian Government Part X - international liner cargo shipping Part XI - application of ACL as a law of the Commonwealth Part XIB - anti-competitive conduct in telecommunications Part XIC - access to services for telecommunications Part XICA - prohibited conduct in the energy market Schedule 2 - The Australian Consumer Law - misleading or deceptive conduct, unconscionable conduct, unfair practices, conditions and warranties, product safety and information, liability of manufacturers for goods with safety defects offences, country of origin representations. Exceptions under Commonwealth, state and territory legislation Some Commonwealth, state and territory Acts allow conduct that would normally contravene the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 . Section 51(1) of the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 permits this conduct if it is specifically authorised under those other Acts. See also Competition and Consumer Act 2010 on Federal Register of Legislation Competition and Consumer Act 2010 on Au
perturbation.justification: The original contract text stipulates that the Recipient must treat Confidential Information with the 'same degree of care as Recipient accords its own confidential information'. The changed text replaces this objective standard with 'degree of care as Recipient deems reasonable', thus introducing a subjective standard. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the potential conflict with the implied duty of good faith and reasonable care. Snippet 2 from ACCC mentions that the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 (CCA) 'covers most areas of the market: the relationships between suppliers, wholesalers, retailers, and consumers. Its purpose is to enhance the welfare of Australians by promoting fair trading and competition, and through the provision of consumer protections.' By allowing the Recipient to determine what is 'reasonable', the modified text weakens consumer protection and potentially undermines the purpose of the CCA, which implies a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the Commonwealth of Australia and by the laws of the St ate of Victoria; as such laws are applied to agreem ents entered into and to be performed entirely within Au stralia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed in all respects by the laws of the State of Delaware, United States; as such laws are applied to agreements entered into and to be performed entirely within the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the governing law to Delaware law, United States, conflicts with the jurisdiction where the Q Society of Australia Inc. is located and operates. This could create jurisdictional disputes if the contract is breached and legal action is pursued, as the contract now specifies a foreign jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Private International Law (Choice of Law)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws outlines the criteria for enforcing choice-of-law provisions. Changing the governing law to Delaware, when the contract has a substantial connection to Australia, introduces a conflict. Australian courts might not enforce the Delaware choice-of-law clause if it violates public policy or has no reasonable relationship to the parties or the transaction. Moreover, this clause will make it more difficult for Q Society to seek relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies Delaware law (United States) as the governing law, while the original text specified the laws of the Commonwealth of Australia and the State of Victoria. The "conflict of laws" definition from the scraped snippet indicates a difference between the laws of two jurisdictions connected to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. By changing the governing law to Delaware when the contract is substantially connected to Australia, a conflict of laws is introduced, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: A breach of any of the promises or agreements co ntained herein will result in irreparable and continuing damage to the Society fo r which there will be no adequate remedy at law, an d the Society shall be entitled to injunctive relief and/ or a decree for specific performance, and such othe r relief as may be proper (including monetary damages if approp riate).
perturbation.changed_text: A breach of any of the promises or agreements co ntained herein will result in irreparable and continuing damage to the Society fo r which there will be no adequate remedy at law, an d the Society shall be entitled to injunctive relief at the Recipient's discretion, and such othe r relief as may be proper (including monetary damages if approp riate).
perturbation.explanation: By giving the Recipient discretion over injunctive relief, the contract contradicts the equitable principles governing such relief. Injunctive relief is typically determined by the court based on the facts and circumstances, not at the discretion of the breaching party. This will be unenforceable under Australian law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Injunctive relief is at the discretion of the court and based on the principles of fairness and equity. Australian courts decide based on the damages of the injured party, and the overall balance of justice. The clause that says 'injunctive relief at the Recipient's discretion' is unenforceable because courts will always assess if equitable relief is just in the given circumstance, irrespective of the contract.
perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates that the Society is entitled to injunctive relief, but the changed version grants the Recipient discretion over that relief. The Law Explanation states that injunctive relief is at the discretion of the court, based on fairness and equity. The change directly contradicts the principle that courts, not the breaching party, determine equitable relief. Scraped Law Snippet 1, though not directly stating this principle, is related to court procedures and determination of equitable relief. This misalignment of who determines injunctive relief (the Recipient in the changed text versus the court according to legal principles) creates a legal contradiction because it attempts to give the breaching party control over a remedy that is traditionally under the purview of the court. The altered clause would likely be unenforceable.
